definition: The process of learning and gaining knowledge and skills.
examples: Education helps people find better jobs.
Every child deserves a good education.
She received her education in Ireland.
Education can change people's lives.
He works in the field of education.
notes: Usually uncountable. We say 'an education' when talking about a particular type of education.

definition: The act of traveling from one place to another or a process of personal development.
examples: Our English journey starts today.
The journey took nearly six hours.
They went on a long journey across Europe.
Learning a language is a lifelong journey.
She shared her journey of becoming a doctor.
notes: Can be used literally (travel) or figuratively (life experience, learning process).

definition: To deal with information, requests, or materials in an organized way.
examples: We will process all the applications within two days.
The system processes thousands of orders every day.
It takes time to process new information.
Your payment is being processed.
The computer can process data very quickly.
notes: Common in business and technology contexts.

definition: To give your time, effort, or attention completely to something.
examples: I dedicate my first novel to my father.
She dedicated her life to helping others.
He dedicated many years to scientific research.
You need to dedicate time to learning English.
The book is dedicated to all teachers.
notes: Often used with 'to': dedicate something to someone; dedicate yourself to something.

definition: Fair treatment of people according to what is right or lawful.
examples: He dedicated his life to fighting for justice.
The victims demanded justice.
Everyone should be treated with justice and respect.
The court seeks justice for all citizens.
She has a strong sense of justice.
notes: Usually uncountable.

definition: Kindness and forgiveness shown toward someone whom you could punish.
examples: Volunteer work requires mercy and compassion.
The prisoner asked for mercy.
They showed mercy to the defeated enemy.
May Allah have mercy on him.
The judge decided to show mercy.
notes: Common in religious and legal contexts.

definition: A feeling of sympathy and a desire to help people who are suffering.
examples: Volunteer work requires mercy and compassion.
She treated her patients with compassion.
Compassion is one of his greatest qualities.
Children need love and compassion.
He showed compassion toward the homeless.
notes: Usually uncountable.

definition: Good for your health or morally good and positive.
examples: My mother always buys fresh and wholesome food.
The children enjoyed a wholesome family environment.
They try to eat wholesome meals.
It is a wholesome activity for young people.
She prefers wholesome ingredients.
notes: Often used with food, lifestyle, and family activities.

definition: To say that something is true, especially when it is difficult or embarrassing to say.
examples: I admit that I was wrong.
He admitted making a mistake.
She didn't want to admit that she needed help.
I have to admit that the movie was excellent.
They finally admitted the truth.
notes: Often followed by 'that' or by a gerund (-ing form).

definition: The careful study of a subject to discover new information.
examples: We did a small research project in class.
The company is conducting market research.
She spent years doing research on climate change.
More research is needed in this area.
The findings are based on recent research.
notes: Usually uncountable. We say 'do research' rather than 'make research'.

definition: Deep respect for God and a sincere desire to do what is right.
examples: Piety is shown not only in worship but also in good character.
His piety inspired many people around him.
She tried to live a life of piety and kindness.
Many people admire her piety and humility.
Piety should be reflected in both words and actions.
notes: Often used in religious contexts to describe devotion and righteous behavior.

definition: To be owned by someone or to be a member of a group.
examples: This book belongs to my brother.
These keys belong to me.
She belongs to a local sports club.
That phone doesn't belong to me.
We all want to belong to a community.
notes: Always followed by 'to'.

definition: Deserving respect because of honesty, integrity, or good actions.
examples: He is an honorable man who always keeps his promises.
Helping others is an honorable thing to do.
She made an honorable decision.
Everyone respected his honorable behavior.
It is honorable to admit your mistakes.
notes: British spelling: honourable.

definition: A large amount of money, possessions, or valuable resources.
examples: Wealth alone does not guarantee happiness.
He accumulated great wealth through his business.
Good health is a form of wealth.
She inherited a considerable amount of wealth.
Some people spend their lives chasing wealth.
notes: Usually an uncountable noun.

definition: To think carefully about something or to regard something in a particular way.
examples: Please consider all the options before making a decision.
I am considering changing my job.
Many people consider him an excellent teacher.
Have you considered moving to another city?
We should consider the long-term effects of this decision.
notes: Can mean both 'think about' and 'regard as'.

definition: Morally good and fair, especially according to religious or ethical principles.
examples: He tried to live a righteous life.
A righteous person treats others with honesty and mercy.
She made a righteous decision even though it was difficult.
Many people respected him for his righteous character.
Righteous actions should come from sincere intentions.
notes: Often used in religious or moral contexts. It can describe a person, action, or way of life.

definition: A person; a member of the human race.
examples: Every human being deserves respect.
A human being needs food, water, and care.
She believes every human being has value.
We should treat each human being with kindness.
No human being is perfect.
notes: More formal or emphatic than 'person'.

definition: All space, time, matter, and energy, including all stars, planets, and galaxies.
examples: The universe is incredibly vast.
Scientists study how the universe began.
There are billions of galaxies in the universe.
Some people look at the universe and think about its Creator.
We still know very little about the universe.
notes: Usually used with 'the': the universe.

definition: To feel pleased and satisfied because of something you or someone else has done or because of who someone is.
examples: I am proud of your progress.
She is proud of her children.
You should be proud of your hard work.
He was proud to help his family.
They are proud of their culture.
notes: Often used with 'of': be proud of someone or something. It can be positive, but 'too proud' can mean arrogant.

definition: Strong belief or trust in God, a religion, a person, or an idea.
examples: Faith gives many people strength.
She has strong faith in Allah.
He never lost faith during difficult times.
Their faith taught them to be patient and kind.
I have faith that things will improve.
notes: Can mean religious belief or trust in someone or something.

definition: To successfully reach a goal or accomplish something through effort.
examples: She worked hard to achieve her goals.
Our team achieved another important milestone.
You can achieve anything if you stay consistent.
He finally achieved his dream of becoming a doctor.
We achieved better performance after the update.
notes: Common collocations: achieve a goal, achieve success, achieve a milestone.

definition: To give someone suggestions or recommendations about what they should do.
examples: I advise you to back up your files.
She advised me on my career choices.
The doctor advised him to get more rest.
Can you advise me on this problem?
My manager advised us to finish the task early.
notes: Common patterns: advise someone; advise someone to do something; advise someone on something.

definition: To have an influence on someone or something.
examples: The weather affected our plans.
Delays could affect our workflow.
The promotion might affect customer behavior.
Lack of sleep affects your concentration.
This decision will affect everyone in the team.
notes: Usually used as a verb. Don't confuse it with 'effect' (noun).

definition: To have the same opinion as someone or to accept something.
examples: I completely agree with you.
We agreed on the final design.
They agreed to meet next Friday.
Everyone agreed that it was a good idea.
Do you agree with this decision?
notes: Common collocations: agree with someone, agree on something, agree to do something.

definition: To let someone do something or to make something possible.
examples: The teacher doesn't allow us to use phones during the test.
This app allows users to save their progress.
My parents allowed me to stay out late.
The budget doesn't allow us to hire more people.
Shopify allows developers to build custom themes.
notes: Common patterns: allow someone to do something; allow something.

definition: To examine something carefully in order to understand it or draw conclusions.
examples: We need to analyze the latest technologies.
The team analyzed the customer feedback.
Before making a decision, analyze all the available data.
The developer analyzed the bug before fixing it.
Scientists analyze results very carefully.
notes: Common collocations: analyze data, analyze results, analyze a situation, analyze performance.

definition: To become visible or to seem to be a certain way.
examples: He appeared before us without warning.
She appeared tired after the meeting.
A new message appeared on my screen.
The issue appeared after the latest deployment.
Everything appeared to be working correctly.
notes: Can mean 'become visible' or 'seem'. Common pattern: appear to be.

definition: To use something in practice or to formally ask for something such as a job or course.
examples: Apply what you've learned today.
You should apply pressure to stop the bleeding.
She applied for a new job.
These rules apply to everyone.
I applied the same solution to another project.
notes: Common collocations: apply pressure, apply knowledge, apply for a job, apply to someone/something.

definition: To recognize the value of someone or something, or to be grateful for it.
examples: I really appreciate your help.
We appreciate your honest feedback.
She appreciates everything her parents have done for her.
I appreciate you taking the time to explain it.
Over time, the value of the house appreciated.
notes: Can also mean 'increase in value'. Common phrase: I appreciate it.

definition: To plan or organize something so that it happens in a particular way.
examples: Let's arrange a meeting for tomorrow.
We arranged to hang out this weekend.
She arranged the books on the shelf.
Can you arrange transportation to the airport?
The manager arranged everything before the event.
notes: Common collocations: arrange a meeting, arrange transportation, arrange to do something.

definition: To help someone do something or support them in completing a task.
examples: She assisted me with the project.
The support team assisted customers with technical issues.
He assisted in organizing the conference.
Can you assist me with this task?
The nurse assisted the doctor during the operation.
notes: More formal than 'help'. Common collocations: assist someone with something; assist in doing something.

definition: To believe something is true without proof, or to take responsibility for something.
examples: I assumed you would be busy.
Don't assume that everyone agrees with you.
She assumed responsibility for the mistake.
We assumed the meeting would start on time.
Never assume anything without checking the facts.
notes: Can mean 'suppose' or 'take responsibility'. Common collocation: assume responsibility.

definition: To go to an event, meeting, class, or other organized activity.
examples: I attended the meeting yesterday.
Thousands of people attended the event.
She attends English classes every week.
Will you attend the conference next month?
Everyone is expected to attend the training session.
notes: Common collocations: attend a meeting, attend an event, attend school, attend a conference.

definition: To stay away from something or prevent something from happening.
examples: I want to avoid talking about it.
Try to avoid making the same mistake.
She avoids eating too much sugar.
We left early to avoid traffic.
He avoided answering the question.
notes: Usually followed by a noun or a verb ending in '-ing'. Example: avoid doing something.

definition: To keep different parts or activities in the correct proportion.
examples: It's important to balance work and life.
She balances her studies with a part-time job.
It's hard to balance both responsibilities.
He struggled to balance family and work.
Good leaders know how to balance different priorities.
notes: Common collocations: balance work and life, balance responsibilities, keep your balance.

definition: To think that something is true or to have confidence in someone or something.
examples: I believe you.
I believe it's a bad idea.
She believes that hard work always pays off.
Do you believe in yourself?
Many people believe that practice makes perfect.
notes: Common patterns: believe someone; believe that...; believe in someone/something.

definition: To say or think that someone is responsible for something bad.
examples: Don't blame me for that.
I blame him for missing the meeting.
She blamed the bad weather for the delay.
It's easy to blame others for your mistakes.
Nobody blamed him for the accident.
notes: Common collocation: blame someone for something.

definition: To take something from someone with the intention of returning it later.
examples: Can I borrow your pen?
He borrowed some money from his friend.
I borrowed a book from the library.
May I borrow your laptop until tomorrow?
She borrowed my charger before the meeting.
notes: Don't confuse 'borrow' with 'lend'. You borrow something FROM someone; someone lends something TO you.

definition: To damage something so it no longer works, or to fail to keep a rule, promise, or agreement.
examples: Be careful not to break the glass.
He broke his promise.
Don't break the rules.
She accidentally broke her phone.
The company didn't break the contract.
notes: Has many meanings. Common collocations: break a promise, break the rules, break the law, break a record.

definition: To determine an amount, number, or result by using mathematics or careful thinking.
examples: We calculated the total cost of the project.
I couldn't calculate how much time I needed.
The app automatically calculates the price.
Can you calculate the distance between these cities?
Engineers calculate everything very carefully.
notes: Common collocations: calculate the cost, calculate the time, calculate the distance.

definition: To make something different or to become different.
examples: She decided to change her hair color.
I changed my mind after talking to you.
Technology changes very quickly.
We need to change our plans because of the weather.
The update changed the way the application works.
notes: Common collocations: change your mind, change your plans, change your behavior, change your appearance.

definition: To decide which person or thing you want from several options.
examples: She chose a career in medicine.
It's difficult to choose the right profession.
You can choose any color you like.
He chose to stay at home.
Choose the option that works best for you.
notes: Irregular verb: choose → chose → chosen. Common collocations: choose a career, choose a profession, choose wisely.

definition: To say that something is true, often without providing proof.
examples: He claimed that he knew her.
She claimed she could speak five languages.
The company claims its product is the fastest.
He claimed that the mistake wasn't his fault.
Several witnesses claimed they saw the accident.
notes: Common pattern: claim that... Can also mean to officially request something that belongs to you.

definition: To share information, thoughts, or feelings with other people.
examples: Please communicate your concerns clearly.
Good leaders communicate effectively.
We communicate with our clients every day.
It's important to communicate honestly.
The app allows teams to communicate more efficiently.
notes: Common collocations: communicate clearly, communicate effectively, communicate with someone.

definition: To examine two or more things to see how they are similar or different.
examples: Always compare prices before buying.
Let's compare the two solutions.
It's unfair to compare yourself with others.
The report compares this year's results with last year's.
We compared several frameworks before making a decision.
notes: Common collocations: compare prices, compare results, compare A with B, compare A to B.

definition: To say that you are unhappy or dissatisfied about something.
examples: Why do you always complain about everything?
Several customers complained about the delay.
She never complains about hard work.
He complained that the room was too cold.
There's no point in complaining if you don't try to solve the problem.
notes: Common patterns: complain about something; complain that...; complain to someone.

definition: To finish something successfully so that nothing is left to do.
examples: We completed the project ahead of schedule.
She completed all her homework before dinner.
Please complete this form before the meeting.
The team completed the migration successfully.
I hope to complete this course by the end of the month.
notes: Common collocations: complete a project, complete a task, complete a form, complete a course.

definition: To give all your attention to something.
examples: Please concentrate on your work.
I couldn't concentrate because of the noise.
She concentrated on preparing for the exam.
Let's concentrate on solving this problem first.
It's easier to concentrate in a quiet room.
notes: Common collocations: concentrate on something, concentrate fully, concentrate your attention.

definition: To think carefully about something before making a decision or to regard something in a particular way.
examples: Please consider all your options before deciding.
I am considering changing my job.
Many people consider him an excellent teacher.
Have you considered moving to another city?
We should consider the long-term effects of this decision.
notes: Can mean 'think about' or 'regard as'. Common collocation: consider your options.

definition: To make someone believe something or persuade them to do something.
examples: She convinced me to join the team.
Can you convince him that this is the best solution?
The evidence convinced the jury of his innocence.
It wasn't easy to convince my parents.
He finally convinced everyone to support the idea.
notes: Common patterns: convince someone to do something; convince someone of something; convince someone that...
